name: codex_autonomous_build_capability
intent: Equip Codex with the ability to bootstrap a multi-thread software delivery workflow from a design document and a runnable environment, without forcing immediate execution.

can_do: Read a GitHub-hosted research pack before planning work.
Convert a design document into a requirement pack and acceptance criteria.
Create a multi-thread workflow for planning, implementation, review, testing, and runtime validation.
Operate with stage gates, handoff artifacts, and failure rollback rules.
Delay execution until explicit activation conditions are met.
cannot_assume: That design documents are complete or unambiguous.
That tests passing means the system is production-ready.
That runtime environments will work without validation.
That full autonomous software delivery is already perfectly reliable.

bootstrap.fallback_rule: If GitHub file tools are unavailable, use raw URLs. If raw URLs are unavailable, request the materials explicitly before planning.

allowed_without_execution: Read and summarize the research pack.
Prepare role definitions, stage gates, and artifact templates.
Explain how future execution would work.

required_inputs_for_future_execution.name: design_document
required_inputs_for_future_execution.description: The source document that defines product scope, requirements, constraints, and expected behavior.

required_inputs_for_future_execution.name: runnable_environment
required_inputs_for_future_execution.description: A real execution environment where setup, tests, and runtime validation can be performed.

role_rules: Implementation threads must have explicit file or module boundaries.
Review and testing must be independent from implementation.
Requirement ambiguity must be recorded as assumptions instead of silently resolved.
Shared files require serialized ownership or an explicit merge step.

phases.name: bootstrap
phases.goal: Load research materials and derive reusable execution rules.

phases.name: requirements_normalization
phases.goal: Transform a design document into a structured and testable requirement pack.

phases.name: planning_and_ownership
phases.goal: Create a task graph, dependency plan, and role ownership map.

phases.name: architecture_and_setup
phases.goal: Define the project structure, setup flow, and test strategy.

phases.name: parallel_implementation
phases.goal: Implement scoped workstreams within fixed ownership boundaries.

phases.name: independent_review
phases.goal: Review the implementation for defects, regressions, and requirement drift.

phases.name: test_and_repair_loop
phases.goal: Run tests, repair failures, and verify there is no test hacking or shallow pass behavior.

phases.name: runtime_validation
phases.goal: Verify the system in a real runnable environment.

phases.name: delivery
phases.goal: Produce a final, evidence-backed delivery summary.

stage_gates: Do not enter implementation before requirements are testable.
Do not enter parallel work before ownership boundaries are explicit.
Do not pass review if high-severity findings remain unresolved.
Do not claim success from unit tests alone; require integration and runtime evidence.
Do not continue normal execution when the environment cannot be brought up; switch to failure diagnosis.

research_usage: Use the research pack to shape workflow design, not to mimic paper wording.
Prefer the official paper pages listed in papers.json when citing or verifying claims.
Acknowledge limits from DevEval-style and OSWorld-style findings when discussing autonomy.
execution_safety: Never start project-changing work without explicit execution permission.
Never collapse requirement, implementation, review, and testing into one unchecked thread.
Never hide ambiguity; record assumptions and unresolved questions explicitly.
Never equate green tests with real-world correctness without runtime validation.

future_execution_success_signals: Requirement pack is explicit and testable.
Task graph is complete and non-redundant.
Independent review signs off on major risks.
Tests pass across the intended layers.
Runtime validation proves the real environment works.
Delivery includes evidence, not just claims.
